title: Phase 5 (optional): Spectrum of Hermitian part for parent-Hamiltonian gap
description:
Diagnostic: spectrum of the Hermitian part of the discriminant.

## Deliverable

discriminant_spectrum(L::AbstractMatrix{<:Complex}, gibbs::Hermitian; n_modes::Int=20)

Returns leading eigenvalues/eigenvectors of H = (widetilde{L} + widetilde{L}')/2 (Hermitian, so eigen(Hermitian(...)) is much cheaper than eigen() on widetilde{L}).

## Why

Chen et al. 2025 Thm I.3 framing: when KMS-DB holds, eigvals(H_part) determines the parent Hamiltonian gap, which is the Lindbladian spectral gap up to a known transform. Useful for mixing-time analyses.

## Reuse

- src/diagnostics.jl:165 extract_leading_eigendata pattern
- Phase 3 hermitian_antihermitian_split

## Test

- For KMS-DB Lindbladian: gap of H_part equals |lambda_2(L)| (similarity invariance)
- For non-DB: H_part is still Hermitian, just doesn't equal L spectrum

## Priority

P4 (optional, not blocking for the main verification API)

## Atomic commit

feat(discriminant): add Hermitian-part spectrum for parent-Ham gap
notes: Promoted from P4 -> P2 on 2026-04-30: discriminant_spectrum is needed by the Phase 4 DBV-B test (spectral-gap-of-H-part = spectral-gap-of-L). Land Phase 5 before completing Phase 4 tests, or expose just enough of it inline for the test.

title: Phase 4: verify_detailed_balance public API + DBVerificationResult + tests
description:
Public-facing detailed balance verification API.

## Deliverables

- DBVerificationResult immutable struct:
  antihermitian_norm::Float64       # ||A||_{2->2}
  hermitian_part_gap::Float64
  fixed_point_residual::Float64     # ||widetilde{L}(sqrt(sigma))||
  discriminant_norm::Float64        # ||widetilde{L}|| (for relative ratios)
  is_kms_db::Bool
  atol::Float64

- verify_detailed_balance(L::AbstractMatrix{<:Complex}, gibbs::Hermitian;
                          atol::Float64=1e-10,
                          eps_trunc::Float64=1e-12,
                          solver::Symbol=:dense) -> DBVerificationResult

  solver=:dense uses LinearAlgebra.opnorm (LAPACK SVD) for n<=6 (d^2<=4096)
  solver=:krylov uses iterative singular-value computation on the action  v -> (D-D')v/2  for larger systems (no materialization of A)

## Export

Add to src/QuantumFurnace.jl exports list (line ~49)

## Tests (test/test_discriminant_verification.jl)

- DBV-01: 3-qubit Bohr KMS (matches DMTST-01 setup at test_dm_detailed_balance.jl:11-32):
  ||A||_{2->2} < 1e-12, fixed_point_residual < 1e-12, is_kms_db == true
- DBV-02: same Lindbladian without coherent B term: ||A|| should grow; pin its order of magnitude (sanity check that the Lamb-shift restores KMS-DB)
- DBV-03: Energy/Time domain hierarchy mirroring DMTST-02 (test_dm_detailed_balance.jl:35): ||A|| monotone with the domain hierarchy
- DBV-04: solver=:dense vs solver=:krylov agree to 1e-9 on the N3 fixture

## Reuse

- Phase 1-3 building blocks
- src/qi_tools.jl trace_distance_h / opnorm
- test/test_dm_detailed_balance.jl fixtures and expected hierarchy

## Atomic commit

feat(discriminant): add verify_detailed_balance public API
notes:
## Physics-diagnostic test sweep (locked in by user 2026-04-30)

Run the following over n in {3, 4, 5} (Bohr domain, KMS, all fast):

### DBV-A: KMS-DB with coherent term -> Hermitian discriminant
- Build L_with_coherent via construct_lindbladian (with B coherent term included)
- Assert antihermitian_norm / discriminant_norm < 1e-10
- Assert is_kms_db == true

### DBV-B: spectral gap of H_part equals spectral gap of L (parent-Hamiltonian gap)
- For the same KMS-DB Lindbladian as DBV-A:
- compute lambda_L = eigvals(L); spectral_gap_L = -maximum(real(lambda_L[lambda_L not 0]))   (i.e. |Re(lambda_2(L))|, smallest nonzero |real|)
- compute H_part = (D + D')/2; lambda_H = eigvals(Hermitian(H_part))
- spectral_gap_H = -maximum(lambda_H[lambda_H .< -atol])   (smallest |negative eigenvalue|; H_part is negative semidefinite for KMS-DB)
- Assert |spectral_gap_L - spectral_gap_H| / spectral_gap_L < 1e-9
- This certifies that the Hermitian part *is* the parent Hamiltonian (Chen et al. 2025 Thm I.3)

### DBV-C: omitting coherent term -> nonzero antihermitian part
- Build L_no_coherent (same parameters, but coherent_term=nothing in the construction, or with_coherent=false)
- Assert antihermitian_norm(L_no_coherent) / discriminant_norm(L_no_coherent) > 1e-3 (concrete order-of-magnitude threshold; pin actual value as regression baseline once measured)
- Assert is_kms_db == false

### DBV-D (existing): solver=:dense vs :krylov agree to 1e-9 on N3 fixture

### eps_trunc note

User confirmed (2026-04-30): max beta in practice is 20. For n<=5 this gives sigma_min ~ exp(-100) ~ 1e-43, so sigma^{-1/4} ~ 1e11 — finite, products balance back to physical magnitude. eps_trunc kwarg stays as a defensive net, default 1e-12 will not be triggered in normal use, do not over-engineer around this.

## Status of these tests

DBV-A and DBV-C are restatements of DBV-01/02. DBV-B is the *new* core diagnostic that ties this work to the parent-Hamiltonian framing. It needs the H-part spectrum, so Phase 5's discriminant_spectrum is no longer optional — it becomes a dependency for Phase 4's test.

title: Phase 3: Materialize discriminant + Hermitian/anti-Hermitian split
description:
Build the d^2 x d^2 form of widetilde{L} (used for opnorm and tests up to n=6) and refactor compute_anti_hermitian_defect through the new API.

## Deliverables

- materialize_discriminant(L::AbstractMatrix{<:Complex}, gibbs::Hermitian; eps_trunc::Float64=1e-12) -> Matrix{ComplexF64}
- hermitian_antihermitian_split(D::AbstractMatrix) -> (Matrix, Matrix)
- Refactor src/diagnostics.jl:236 compute_anti_hermitian_defect to call these

## Implementation (minimum-copy)

In column-stacking: widetilde{L}[i,j] = sigma_inv_quarter_kron[i] * L[i,j] * sigma_quarter_kron[j]
where sigma_quarter_kron is built lazily as the outer product of sigma_quarter with itself (length d^2 vector — never a d^2 x d^2 matrix).

This is exactly the kernel already at src/diagnostics.jl:246-249 — extract it.

For the split: compute H = (D + D')/2, A = (D - D')/2 in place via two BLAS axpy passes; or expose the action  v -> (D - D')v / 2  as a closure for Krylov opnorm.

## Reuse

- src/diagnostics.jl:236-260 compute_anti_hermitian_defect (the diagonal-scaling kernel)
- src/qi_tools.jl:12 hermitianize! for cleanup of the Hermitian part

## Tests

- Spectrum invariance: eigvals(materialize_discriminant(L, gibbs)) ≈ eigvals(L) to 1e-10 (similarity transform)
- Self-adjointness via second equality of thesis Eq. eq:discriminant: D(sigma, L)^dag = D(sigma, L^dag)
- compute_anti_hermitian_defect after refactor produces the same numbers as before on N3 fixture (regression test against current values)

## Atomic commit

refactor(discriminant): add materialization + split, route diagnostics through it

title: Phase 2: Action-style apply_discriminant! on dxd operators
description:
Closure-based discriminant action — never forms the d^2 x d^2 superoperator.

## Deliverable

apply_discriminant!(out::AbstractMatrix, X::AbstractMatrix,
                    lindblad_action!::F,
                    sigma_quarter::AbstractVector,
                    sigma_inv_quarter::AbstractVector,
                    buffers::DiscriminantBuffers) where {F<:Function}

Computes  out = sigma^{-1/4} . L(sigma^{1/4} X sigma^{1/4}) . sigma^{-1/4}

## Implementation notes

- sigma^{1/4} X sigma^{1/4}: use lmul!(Diagonal(sigma_quarter), tmp) then rmul!(tmp, Diagonal(sigma_quarter))
- L(.) call: invoke lindblad_action!(buffers.work2, buffers.work1) — generic over any apply_lindbladian! closure from src/krylov_matvec.jl:91
- sigma^{-1/4} L(...) sigma^{-1/4}: same lmul!/rmul! with sigma_inv_quarter
- Zero allocations on hot path

## Reuse

- src/krylov_matvec.jl:91 apply_lindbladian! signature pattern (closure with !-form)
- LinearAlgebra.lmul!/rmul!/Diagonal

## Test

- For N3 fixture, build dense L via construct_lindbladian, then for many random X verify
  apply_discriminant!(out, X, ...) ≈ reshape(materialized_D * vec(X), d, d)
  to 1e-12 (Phase 3 will provide the materialized D, but for this phase a reference D can be built ad hoc in the test)
- @allocated check: zero allocations after warmup

## Atomic commit

feat(discriminant): add closure-style action on operators

title: Phase 1: Fractional Gibbs powers + DiscriminantBuffers in src/discriminant.jl
description:
Create src/discriminant.jl with the foundational pieces.

## Deliverables

- DiscriminantBuffers{T<:Complex} immutable struct: 3 dxd workspace matrices for the action path
- gibbs_fractional_powers(gibbs::Hermitian; eps_trunc::Float64=1e-12)
  - Returns (sigma_quarter::Vector{T}, sigma_inv_quarter::Vector{T}, sigma_half::Vector{T})
  - Diagonal-Gibbs only in v1 (Bohr/Energy/Time); take diag(gibbs).^(±0.25)
  - Floor any sigma_i < eps_trunc to avoid sigma^{-1/4} divergence at low T

## Reuse

- src/hamiltonian.jl:40 HamHam.gibbs (already a Hermitian, diagonal in eigenbasis, see _gibbs_in_eigen at line 49)
- src/diagnostics.jl:240 eps_trunc=1e-12 pattern

## Test (test/test_discriminant.jl, new file)

- 'fractional powers reproduce sigma': sigma_quarter.^4 ≈ diag(gibbs) to 1e-14
- 'inverse identity': sigma_quarter .* sigma_inv_quarter ≈ ones to 1e-14
- 'eps_trunc behaviour': low-T case where some sigma_i ~ 1e-20 doesn't blow up sigma_inv_quarter

## Atomic commit

feat(discriminant): add fractional Gibbs powers and buffer struct

title: Detailed-balance verification: quantum discriminant tools (KMS)
description:
Implement Julia tools to compute the KMS quantum discriminant of a Lindbladian and verify detailed balance.

## Formula (thesis Eq. eq:discriminant; identical to Chen et al. 2025)

D(sigma, L) = sigma^{-1/4} L(sigma^{1/4} . sigma^{1/4}) sigma^{-1/4}

KMS detailed balance is equivalent to D being Hermitian under the Hilbert-Schmidt inner product. In column-stacking vec the dxd^2 matrix realisation is

D = (sigma^{-1/4} kron sigma^{-1/4}) L (sigma^{1/4} kron sigma^{1/4})

(no transpose on diagonal sigma factors in eigenbasis). Hermitian/anti-Hermitian split: H = (D + D^dag)/2, A = (D - D^dag)/2. KMS-DB iff ||A||_{2->2} = 0.

## Scope decisions (locked in by user 2026-04-29)

- KMS only (no GNS in v1)
- Target n <= 6 (d^2 <= 4096), with action-only path keeping n=7 viable
- Diagonal-Gibbs domains (Bohr, Energy, Time) in v1; TrotterDomain deferred
- Norm: opnorm (2->2) per thesis Def. def:approx-db; dense SVD by default, Krylov fallback for large systems
- New file src/discriminant.jl; refactor src/diagnostics.jl::compute_anti_hermitian_defect through it
- Minimum-copy strategy: materialize widetilde{L} once; compute ||A||_2 via Krylov on the anti-Hermitization action, never form A as a separate matrix

## Reuse (codebase audit)

- src/qi_tools.jl: column-stacking vec convention (line 69), _kron! (21), hermitianize! (12), opnorm wrappers
- src/hamiltonian.jl:40 HamHam.gibbs::Hermitian (diagonal in eigenbasis, line 49 _gibbs_in_eigen)
- src/diagnostics.jl:236 compute_anti_hermitian_defect — direct prior art; gets refactored into new API
- src/krylov_matvec.jl:91 apply_lindbladian! — closure for action-style discriminant
- test/test_dm_detailed_balance.jl: DMTST-01 (3-qubit Bohr KMS) and DMTST-02 (hierarchy) become golden tests
- test/test_helpers.jl:160 N3_HAM, N3_JUMPS, N3_GIBBS fixtures

## Phases (child issues)

1. Fractional Gibbs powers + DiscriminantBuffers struct
2. Action-style apply_discriminant! on dxd operators (closure, no superoperator)
3. Materialization + Hermitian/anti-Hermitian split (refactor compute_anti_hermitian_defect)
4. Public verify_detailed_balance API + DBVerificationResult + tests on DMTST-01/02
5. (Optional) Spectrum of Hermitian part for parent-Hamiltonian gap

title: Plot 4: OFT Gaussian smearing around two Bohr frequencies
description:
Visualize what 'smearing around Bohr frequencies' means for the OFT.

x-axis: energy ω. Two arbitrary Bohr frequencies ν_1, ν_2 are marked.

For each ν_i, overlay the Gaussian filter f̂(ω - ν_i) with the canonical Chen et al. parameters (β = 10, σ = 1/β):

    f̂(ω) = (2πσ²)^{-1/4} exp(-ω² / (4σ²))

The two Gaussians should overlap a bit, to visually motivate why the OFT smearing makes it ambiguous which Bohr frequency a given ω value belongs to — i.e. the source of the Gaussian smearing error in the Kossakowski matrix.

Output: drafts/plots/oft_smearing.{pdf,png}.
Script: scripts/plot_oft_smearing.jl
notes: Final polish (2026-04-29): title 'filtering' (not 'smearing'); ν₁=-0.10, ν₂=+0.20 (3σ separation); legend top-left fontsize 7; ν labels size-16 italic math anchored at y=1.5 just right of dashed lines; width arrow at \hat{f}(0)/e height annotated '4/β' at x=ν₁+0.04 to clear the dashed line; overlap region shaded deepplum. Plotting f̂ amplitude (not |f̂|²) for thesis consistency with line 53–56.
close_reason: Wrote scripts/plot_oft_smearing.jl. Plots two L²-normalized Gaussian filters \hat{f}(\omega - \nu_i) with the canonical Chen et al. parameters (β=10, σ=1/β=0.1) at ν₁=-0.10 and ν₂=+0.20 (separation 3σ ≈ 0.9·FWHM). Overlap region shaded with deepplum to mark the smearing-induced ambiguity. Output drafts/plots/oft_smearing.{pdf,png}.

This is where the regime-map plots get generated (regime cells × known classical/quantum runtimes × advantage status).

title: Thesis 'Review' chapter: quantum vs classical Gibbs sampling — where quantum could first beat classical
description:
# Thesis "Review" Chapter — Quantum vs Classical Gibbs Sampling

## Goal

Write a thesis chapter currently called **Review** that gathers the state of the art on Gibbs sampling — both quantum and classical — and frames the central question:

> **Where could quantum Gibbs sampling first beat a classical sampler?**

The chapter must give a complete-enough overview that a reader can locate, on a regime map, where quantum advantage is plausible, where it is unlikely, and where the answer is currently unknown.

## What to cover

**Quantum side** (primary focus):
- Chen et al. KMS detailed-balance Lindbladian (the main thesis subject)
- Other quantum Gibbs samplers across regimes:
  - **Commuting Hamiltonians** — efficient classical baselines exist; what does quantum buy?
  - **Noncommuting Hamiltonians** — where Chen-style KMS DB lives
  - **Free fermionic / quasi-free** systems
  - **Bosonic** systems (if literature supports)
  - **High temperature** (rapid mixing regimes)
  - **Low temperature** (Ding et al., gapped ground-state-adjacent regimes)
  - **Lower bounds / no-go** results

**Classical side**:
- The methods practitioners would actually use today: Glauber/Metropolis MCMC, cluster algorithms (Swendsen-Wang, Wolff), tensor networks (DMRG/PEPS at finite T), QMC where applicable, parallel tempering, …
- Their proven runtimes / mixing times for the regimes above.

## Quantum-classical comparison framing

Every entry must answer: **why is the quantum version harder or easier here, and what is the parameter gap (poly / super-poly / unknown)?** Sources of the difference (sign problem, noncommutativity, KMS condition, locality of Lindblad operators, etc.) must be identified.

## Visualization

The chapter should include figures that **visualize regime cells** — temperature × commutativity × locality × particle statistics — and indicate in each cell:
- the best known classical runtime
- the best known quantum runtime
- whether quantum advantage is proven, conjectured, ruled out, or unknown

## Infrastructure already in place

- **`/lit-review`** slash command (`.claude/skills/lit-review/SKILL.md`) — forks the **`lit-reviewer`** agent (`.claude/agents/lit-reviewer.md`) on a paper PDF / arXiv ID / title.
- **Output**: `drafts/literature-review/<paper-slug>.md` — short, bullet-heavy, regime-tagged via mandatory YAML frontmatter (`temperature`, `commutativity`, `locality`, `particle-statistics`, `paradigm`, `quantum-or-classical`, `result-type`, `key-scaling`, `related`).
- Per-paper reviews are deliberately short (~1.5 pages) so a later synthesis pass can ingest many.

## Plan

1. **Build the corpus**: run `/lit-review` on every relevant paper in `supplementary-informations/` and on the additional classical-side references that need to be sourced. Each call produces one regime-tagged review.
2. **Add a `/lit-synthesize` skill** (not yet built): takes a regime filter (e.g. `"high-T noncommuting"`), reads matching frontmatter cells, and composes a regime overview draft. This is where the regime-map plots come in.
3. **Compose the chapter**: thesis-writer pulls the synthesized regime overviews into the Review chapter prose, with the regime-map figure(s) as anchors.

## Initial paper list (priority candidates)

From `supplementary-informations/` already on disk:
- Chen et al. 2023 — Quantum Thermal State Preparation
- Chen et al. 2025 — An efficient and exact noncommutative quantum Gibbs sampler
- Ding et al. 2024 — Efficient quantum Gibbs samplers with KMS
- Ding et al. 2024 — Polynomial-Time Preparation of Low-Temperature Gibbs states
- Ding et al. 2025 — End-to-End Efficient Quantum Thermal and Ground State Preparation
- Capel et al. 2025 — Quasi-optimal Sampling from Gibbs States via Non-commutative Optimal Transport Metrics
- Fang et al. 2026 — Quantum Gibbs sampling through the detectability lemma
- Kochanowski et al. 2024 — Rapid thermalization of dissipative many-body dynamics
- Lin 2025 — Dissipative Preparation of Many-Body Quantum States
- Ramkumar & Soleimanifar 2024 — Mixing time for random sparse Hamiltonians
- Scandi & Alhambra 2025 — Thermalization in open many-body systems and KMS detailed balance
- Rouzé — Functional inequalities in quantum information theory

To be sourced (classical and additional quantum):
- Modern classical MCMC mixing-time results (Glauber on lattice models, high-T results)
- Cluster algorithms (Swendsen-Wang/Wolff) state-of-the-art
- Tensor-network finite-T methods (Verstraete et al., METTS)
- QMC sign-problem-free results (Bravyi-Gosset stoquastic, …)
- Quantum lower bounds for Gibbs sampling (if any)
- Free-fermion-specific results

title: EnergyDomain pick_transition: handle a=0, s>0 with smooth gamma
notes:
## Problem

After Phase 45 (commits 9ddd782, e405763, 1ffba44), the `(a=0, s>0)` path uses the smooth eta-regularized `b_+(t)` from `_compute_b_plus_metro` (consistent with thesis 2_methods.tex eq. 524). However, `pick_transition` in `src/energy_domain.jl` still returns the **kinky** Metropolis γ_0 for any `a=0` regardless of `s`:

```julia
# src/energy_domain.jl:19-21 (KMS)
elseif config.a == 0
    return exp(-config.beta * max(w + config.beta * config.sigma^2 / 2, 0.0))
```

Same pattern at lines 39-41 (KMS line in 2-arg variant), 76-81 (closure variant), 133-139 (GNS variant).

So in the thesis-main regime `(a=0, s>0)`:
- `b_+(t)` is smooth (correct)
- `γ(ω)` is kinky (wrong — should be the smooth Metropolis weight)

## Where it matters

1. **EnergyDomain construction** of `gamma_norm_factor` and the integrand of L (transition × Gaussian filter × Gaussian filter). Direct use of γ; the kinky form yields a different Lindbladian than the smooth one.
2. **BohrDomain construction** via `_pick_alpha_kms` — calls `create_alpha`, which DOES handle smooth Metro at a=0 since it just plugs values into the erfc form. But the `f` function in `create_f` uses `tanh(...) * alpha`, fine.
3. **TimeDomain/TrotterDomain** — `gamma_norm_factor = 1/maximum(transition.(energy_labels))` (`furnace_utensils.jl:108`). Using kinky γ for normalization would over-normalize relative to smooth γ values (which are pointwise smaller). For small `s` (~0.1-0.5) the max is similar so this is small, but not zero.

## Fix

Per `metro-funcs.md` line 13 and the energy-domain branch structure, at `a=0` the transition should be:

γ_s(ω) = γ_0(ω) · (1/2) · [erfc(z_-) + e^{4√(AB)} · erfc(z_+)]

where γ_0 is the kinky Metropolis (current code's `a=0` return), and the bracket factor approaches 2 as s → 0 (recovering γ_0).

Concretely, unify the three branches in `pick_transition`:
```julia
sqrtA = sqrt(config.beta / 4) * sqrt(4 * config.a + 1)
sqrtB = sqrt(config.beta / 4) * abs(w + config.beta * config.sigma^2 / 2)
transition_b0 = exp(-2*sqrtA*sqrtB - β*w/2 - β²σ²/4)  # this equals γ_0 at a=0
if config.s == 0
    return transition_b0
else
    u_min = sqrt(config.beta * config.sigma^2 * config.s / 2)  # OR (1+s) — see qf-hzo
    return transition_b0 * (erfc(sqrtA*u_min - sqrtB/u_min) + exp(4*sqrtA*sqrtB)*erfc(sqrtA*u_min + sqrtB/u_min)) / 2
end
```

Apply the same restructuring to all four variants (KMS 2-arg, KMS closure, GNS 2-arg, GNS closure).

## Blocked by

`qf-hzo` (u_min formula choice) — should be resolved first, otherwise we'd need to change this twice.

## Test

After fix: in `test_smooth_metro_eta.jl`, add an assertion that `pick_transition(c, ω)` for a config with `a=0, s=0.4` differs from the kinky form at energies near the kink (`ω ≈ -βσ²/2`), and that `pick_transition(c_s0, ω) ≈ pick_transition(c_smooth_a0, ω)` as `s → 0`.

## Origin

Found 2026-04-28 by code-verifier during Phase 45 review.
Smoke test 2026-04-29 after LOCK cleanup — verifying write path works
close_reason: Commit 25ff241: pick_transition now uses the smooth-erfc branch for any (s != 0, including a=0) in all four variants (KMS/GNS × 2-arg/closure). Validation: trace distance to Gibbs is 6.6e-15 in EnergyDomain at (a=0, s=0.4), confirming γ-α consistency. The O(t₀) residual in TimeDomain is from the t=0 trapezoidal-quadrature interaction with the η-regularized b_+, intrinsic to the time-domain pipeline and unrelated to this fix.

title: Plot 3: Kossakowski matrix α_{ν1,ν2} heatmaps for disordered Heisenberg
description:
Heatmap of the Kossakowski matrix α_{ν1,ν2} to highlight the relevance of off-diagonal entries.

## System
- Disordered Heisenberg Hamiltonian (choose parameters yielding a unique spectrum for simplicity)

## Variants
- Gaussian transition weight
- Smooth Metropolis transition weight

## Temperatures
- β = 5 (higher T)
- β = 20 (lower T)

## Goal
Show how off-diagonal terms become more or less relevant across variants and temperatures.

title: Plot 1: Transition weights γ (Gaussian, kinky Metropolis, smooth Metropolis)
description:
Plot the three transition weights γ used in the thesis on a single figure:
- Gaussian
- Kinky Metropolis
- Smooth Metropolis

## s selection
Choose s such that:
- Large enough to capture the quadrature-error benefits
- Small enough to keep transition rates close to the optimal kinky Metropolis profile

Document the rationale for the chosen s in the plot or its caption.
close_reason: Plot generated at drafts/plots/transition_weights.{pdf,png}: γ_G (Gaussian), γ_M (kinky Metropolis), γ_M^{(s)} (smooth Metropolis, s=0.25), γ_Gl (Glauber). β=10, σ=ω_γ=σ_γ=1/β (Chen et al. convention). Colour scheme to be revisited later.

title: Plot 2: b_+ and b_- for kinky Metropolis and smooth Metropolis
description:
Plot b_+ for:
- Kinky Metropolis
- Smooth Metropolis

Use the same s > 0 as in Plot 1 for visual consistency.
notes:
Also include b_- alongside b_+ — useful to display even if it coincides across variants, for completeness.
Clarification: plot b_±(t) in the time domain (not the frequency-domain γ̂_± or similar).
Produced scripts/plot_b_plus_b_minus.jl: standalone runnable plotting script, mirroring style of scripts/plot_transition_weights.jl. Outputs drafts/plots/b_plus.{pdf,png} (|b_+(t)| for Gaussian/kinky/smooth Metropolis at β=10, σ=1/β, s=0.25, η=0.05) and drafts/plots/b_minus.{pdf,png} (universal b_-(t)). Sanity checks: |b_+|(0) matches L'Hopital limits to machine precision for all three variants; b_-(0)=0 by parity (even∗odd=odd); L¹ norm of b_- (0.83) within Chen analytic bound (2.52). Discontinuity at |t|=η rendered as a true line break by splitting the kinky/smooth curves into three segments. Colours match Plot 1 (slateblue Gauss, bordeaux kinky, deepplum smooth, pinegreen b_-).
Follow-up plot added: scripts/plot_b_plus_l1_vs_eta.jl produces drafts/plots/b_plus_l1_vs_eta.{pdf,png}. Sweeps ‖b_+^{(s,η)}‖_1 vs η for smooth Metropolis (s=0.25), overlays the thesis upper bound (2_methods.tex eq. 528), marks the deterministic-LCU threshold ‖b_+‖_1 = 1. Numerical η_crit ≈ 3.27e-7 vs thesis-bound η_crit ≈ 2.12e-6 (bound looser by ×6.5).
close_reason: Done. scripts/plot_b_plus_b_minus.jl produces drafts/plots/b_plus.{pdf,png} and drafts/plots/b_minus.{pdf,png}. β=10, σ=1/β, s=0.25, η=0.05; sanity checks against L'Hôpital limits and odd-parity of b_- pass to machine precision.

title: Thesis plots: transition weights, b_+ functions, and Kossakowski matrix heatmaps
description:
Three plots needed before the big numerical-analysis chapter plots.

## Plot 1: Transition weights γ
Plot the three transition weights used in the thesis:
- Gaussian
- Kinky Metropolis
- Smooth Metropolis

Choose s such that:
- Large enough to obtain the quadrature-error benefits
- Small enough to keep transition rates close to optimal (kinky Metropolis is proven optimal)

## Plot 2: b_+ functions
Plot b_+ for:
- Kinky Metropolis
- Smooth Metropolis

Use the same s > 0 as in Plot 1 for consistency.

## Plot 3: Kossakowski matrix α_{ν1,ν2} heat maps
- System: disordered Heisenberg Hamiltonian (choose parameters giving a unique spectrum for simplicity)
- Visualize how relevant the off-diagonal entries are (heatmap)
- Compare variants: Gaussian vs Smooth Metropolis
- Compare temperatures: β = 5 (higher T) and β = 20 (lower T) to highlight differences

## Goal
These plots accompany analytical claims in the thesis before moving on to the full numerical-analysis chapter.
notes: Scope update: Plot 2 now includes b_- in addition to b_+.

title: Phase 47: Integration API and Validation — Public API, convenience overloads, end-to-end tests
description:
## Phase 47: Integration API and Validation

**Goal**: Single public API function returning a complete Hamiltonian simulation time budget from HamHam + scalar parameters, with convenience overloads for MixingTimeEstimate chaining.

### Deliverables
1. `compute_simulation_time(ham, r, delta, mixing_time; ...)` returning `SimulationTimeBudget`
   - per_step_time = 2*oft_time + b_time (NOT 2*(oft_time + b_time))
   - n_steps = ceil(mixing_time / delta)
   - total_time = n_steps * per_step_time
2. HamHam convenience overload extracting rescaling_factor and n_qubits automatically
3. MixingTimeEstimate overload extracting mixing_time from estimate
4. Module exports: `SimulationTimeBudget` and `compute_simulation_time`

### Success Criteria
- Formula: per_step = 2*OFT + B (not 2*(OFT+B))
- n_steps = ceil(mixing_time / delta)
- HamHam and MixingTimeEstimate convenience overloads work
- Validation: r=12 on n=3,4 systems → finite, positive, physically plausible results
- `src/simulation_time.jl` included in main module; both names exported

### Key Insight
The per-step cost is 2×OFT + B because OFT appears twice (forward and backward transform) but B appears once. This asymmetry is important for correct resource estimation.

title: Phase 46: B Coherent Term Counting — B term double-sum Ham sim time with KMS/GNS dispatch
description:
## Phase 46: B Coherent Term Counting

**Goal**: Compute the B coherent term Hamiltonian simulation time as a double sum over truncated b-dicts, with correct KMS/GNS dispatch.

### Deliverables
1. `_b_hamiltonian_time(b_minus, b_plus, ...)` computing double sum over truncated dictionaries with separate inner/outer contributions
2. GNS returns exactly 0.0 (no coherent term in GNS construction)
3. Support for all three transition weight functions via correct b_plus variant dispatch

### Success Criteria
- Double sum over b_minus (outer) and b_plus (inner) truncated dictionaries
- B term returns exactly 0.0 for GNS construction
- B term works with all three transition weight functions
- B term result increases with beta (larger beta → more Ham sim time for coherent correction)

### Key Insight
The B term is a KMS-only correction. For GNS it's identically zero. The cost comes from a double sum involving the b-dict entries, each requiring its own Ham sim time.

title: Phase 45: OFT Time Counting — Operator Fourier transform Ham sim time from QPE grid
description:
## Phase 45: OFT Time Counting

**Goal**: Compute the Operator Fourier Transform Hamiltonian simulation time — the dominant cost component — with closed-form validation and all three transition weight functions.

### Deliverables
1. `_oft_hamiltonian_time(r, w0, transition_weights)` computing sum of |t_k| over full 2^r QPE grid
2. Support for Gaussian, Metropolis (a=0), and smooth Metropolis (a>0, b>0) transition weight functions
3. Closed-form sanity check: unweighted QPE grid sum = t0*N^2/4 to machine precision

### Success Criteria
- OFT time is independent of sigma (inner QPE sum doesn't apply filter weights)
- Changing sigma does not change the time sum
- Closed-form validation passes
- Works with all three transition weight function variants

### Key Insight
The OFT time is the sum of |t_k| weighted by transition function values over the QPE grid — it does NOT depend on the filter function (sigma). This is the dominant per-step cost.

title: Phase 44: Struct and Grid Infrastructure — SimulationTimeBudget struct + QPE grid utilities
description:
## Phase 44: Struct and Grid Infrastructure

**Goal**: Result container and QPE grid utilities that all subsequent cost computations build upon.

### Deliverables
1. `SimulationTimeBudget` immutable struct with per-component fields:
   - Cost fields: oft_time, b_time, per_step_time, n_steps, total_time
   - Grid info: r, N, w0, t0, energy_range
   - Physical params: beta, sigma, delta, construction type, n_qubits, rescaling_factor, mixing_time
   - Filter info: filter type (:gaussian, :metropolis, :smooth_metropolis) and parameters
2. `_qpe_grid_info(r, w0)` returning N=2^r, t0 via Fourier relation t0*w0=2π/N, energy range
3. Two-method `show`: compact one-liner + verbose multi-line with formula breakdown

### Success Criteria
- N=2^r for various r values, Fourier relation holds to machine precision
- Struct constructor enforces immutability
- Stores all parameters needed for paper resource estimation tables
- New file: `src/simulation_time.jl` included in main module

### Context
- See `.planning/phases/44-struct-and-grid-infrastructure/44-CONTEXT.md` for design decisions
- No HamHam reference stored in struct (scalars only)
- Times in raw scientific notation

title: v2.2 Ham Sim Time Counting: Build functions that count total Hamiltonian simulation time for Chen's quantum Gibbs sampling algorithm
Resolve u_min formula inconsistency: bohr_domain (1+s) vs energy_domain s
notes:
## Problem

`u_min` (the lower-integration-limit shift in the smooth Metropolis erfc form) is implemented inconsistently across the codebase:

- `src/bohr_domain.jl:96, 130` (`create_alpha`, `create_alpha_gns`):
  ```julia
  u_min = sqrt(beta * sigma^2 * (1 + s) / 2)
  ```
- `src/energy_domain.jl:16, 36, 66, 126` (`pick_transition` variants):
  ```julia
  u_min = sqrt(config.beta * config.sigma^2 * config.s / 2)
  ```

Differ by a factor of `√(1+s)` vs `√s` — at `s=1` they agree up to √2; at small `s` they diverge sharply.

## Doc inconsistency

The thesis source `supplementary-informations/metro-funcs.md` is itself inconsistent:

- **Line 13** (γ definition): integration lower limit is `βσ²(1+b)/2`
- **Line 18** (γ z_± formula): `z_± = √A · √(b βσ²/2) ± √(B / (b βσ²/2))` — uses `b βσ²/2`
- **Line 38** (α z_± formula): `z_± = √A · √(βσ²(1+b)/2) ± √(B / (βσ²(1+b)/2))` — uses `(1+b) βσ²/2`

The integration lower limit on line 13 says `(1+b)`. If line 18 is meant to follow from the substitution `x = u²` (heat-diffusion form), then `u_min = √x_0 = √(βσ²(1+b)/2)`, matching line 38. So **line 18 is likely a typo** (b instead of 1+b).

## Implication

Either:
- (a) `bohr_domain.jl` is correct, `energy_domain.jl` has a typo (use `(1+s)`).
- (b) `energy_domain.jl` is correct, `bohr_domain.jl` has a typo (use `s`).
- (c) The two contexts genuinely differ — e.g., a different change of variables in α vs γ.

User to verify by re-deriving from the thesis, then unify.

## Action

1. Re-derive `u_min` for both the γ (transition weight) and α (Kossakowski matrix) cases from the integral definitions in `metro-funcs.md` line 7-22 and line 30-38.
2. Update `metro-funcs.md` to be self-consistent.
3. Update either `bohr_domain.jl` or `energy_domain.jl` to match the corrected form.
4. Add a regression test that compares the two: at the same `(β, σ, s)`, does the ratio `α(ν,ν) / γ(ν)` match the analytical relation expected from KMS detailed balance?

## Origin

Found 2026-04-28 by code-verifier during Phase 45 review. Pre-existing — not introduced by the rename, just made more visible with a unified parameter name.

## Blocks

`qf-s3b` (the EnergyDomain pick_transition fix needs the canonical u_min formula).
close_reason: Not a bug — different substitutions are correct. γ uses substitution u² = x − βσ²/2, yielding u_min = √(βσ²·s/2) (matches thesis line 302). α uses u² = x, yielding u_min = √(βσ²·(1+s)/2) (matches thesis line 402). Both forms come from the same integral with lower limit βσ²(1+s)/2 (thesis eq:smooth-metro-int-defi line 293), they just differ by which substitution variable is used. metro-funcs.md line 18 vs line 38 was internally inconsistent (likely typo on line 18); thesis 2_methods.tex is the authoritative source and resolves it: γ uses √s, α uses √(1+s). Confirmed by user.
